docs.title: Yifan Liu（刘异凡）
docs.text:
I am a Ph.D. candidate in Condensed Matter Theory at the University of California, Riverside, advised by Prof. Vivek Aji. Prior to joining UCR, I received my B.S. in Physics from Beijing Institute of Technology in 2018. My research interests include strongly correlated electronic systems, moiré materials, and non-Hermitian physics.

I am currently studying twisted bilayer graphene and other moire materials with a focus on electron-phonon coupling and its implications for correlated phases.

Outside of physics, I enjoy hiking, cooking, and classical music.

Email | Google Scholar | GitHub

docs.title: Research Interests
docs.text: Strongly correlated systems Moiré materials Electron-phonon coupling Twisted bilayer graphene and TMDs Non-Hermitian systems

docs.title: Research
docs.text: My research is in theoretical condensed matter physics, with a focus on interaction-driven phenomena in quantum materials. I am particularly interested in moiré systems, electron-phonon coupling, strongly correlated electronic phases, and non-Hermitian effects in quantum systems.

docs.title: Moiré materials and electron-phonon coupling
docs.text: A central part of my research concerns moiré materials and van der Waals heterostructures, especially twisted bilayer systems and transition metal dichalcogenide heterostructures. I study how lattice degrees of freedom couple to electronic states, and how electron-phonon interactions shape optical response, excitation processes, and correlated phases in low-dimensional materials.

docs.title: Strongly correlated phases and ordering phenomena
docs.text: I also work on interaction-driven ordering in lattice models of correlated electrons. My recent work includes bond ordering in flux phases, loop current fluctuations, and related broken-symmetry states. More broadly, I am interested in how interactions generate competing ordered phases and how these phases can be characterized theoretically.

docs.title: Non-Hermitian quantum systems
docs.text: Another direction of my research is non-Hermitian physics. In this context, I study how non-Hermitian structure modifies localization, dynamics, and quantum control in model systems, and how these effects can lead to qualitatively new physical behavior beyond conventional Hermitian settings.
